title: Force Push Detection
spl:
index=devops sourcetype="github:webhook" event="push" forced="true"
| table _time, repository, ref, pusher, forced
description: Force pushes overwrite git history, potentially destroying code and audit trails. Detection ensures accountability.
value: Force pushes overwrite git history, potentially destroying code and audit trails. Detection ensures accountability.
implementation: Parse force push flag from webhook events. Alert on any force push to shared branches. Whitelist expected force pushes (e.g., squash-merge workflows on feature branches). Track frequency per developer.
visualization: Table (force push events), Timeline (force pushes), Single value (force pushes this week).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: GitHub webhook.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Git push events (forced flag).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Parse force push flag from webhook events. Alert on any force push to shared branches. Whitelist expected force pushes (e.g., squash-merge workflows on feature branches). Track frequency per developer.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=devops sourcetype="github:webhook" event="push" forced="true"
| table _time, repository, ref, pusher, forced
```

Understanding this SPL

**Force Push Detection** — Force pushes overwrite git history, potentially destroying code and audit trails. Detection ensures accountability.

Documented **Data sources**: Git push events (forced flag). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): GitHub webhook. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: devops; **sourcetype**: github:webhook. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=devops, sourcetype="github:webhook". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Pipeline stage (see **Force Push Detection**): table _time, repository, ref, pusher, forced


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GitHub audit log. Verify field extractions and index permissions.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (force push events), Timeline (force pushes), Single value (force pushes this week).
grandmaExplanation: We watch for history rewrites on protected branches so we know when someone may have removed audit history or forced code in without the usual checks.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Change.All_Changes
  by All_Changes.user All_Changes.object All_Changes.action span=1h
| sort -count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration on Change.All_Changes before using this tstats search.
knownFalsePositives: Force pushes from approved repo cleanup, history rewrites for license remediation, or scheduled rebases on long-running branches.
